expanded_description:
Fiscal policy refers to changes in government spending (G) and taxes (T) designed to influence aggregate demand, output, and employment. In the IS-LM model, fiscal policy operates through the IS curve: an increase in government spending or a decrease in taxes shifts the IS curve to the right, increasing output at any given interest rate. However, unlike the simple Keynesian cross where interest rates are fixed, in the IS-LM model fiscal policy also affects interest rates through the money market. Higher output increases money demand, which raises interest rates (crowding out), partially offsetting the initial increase in output.

Understanding fiscal policy in the IS-LM model is essential for analyzing how government spending and tax changes affect the economy, evaluating the effectiveness of fiscal stimulus during recessions, and understanding the phenomenon of crowding out. The model shows that the multiplier for fiscal policy is smaller than the simple Keynesian multiplier because of crowding out, and that the effectiveness of fiscal policy depends on the slopes of the IS and LM curves. In a liquidity trap (horizontal LM), crowding out is zero and fiscal policy is very effective. In the classical case (vertical LM), crowding out is complete and fiscal policy is ineffective.

In this section, we will analyze the effects of government spending increases and tax cuts on output and interest rates, derive the fiscal policy multipliers, explore crowding out in detail, examine the balanced budget multiplier, compare the effectiveness of spending increases vs. tax cuts, and discuss the limitations of fiscal policy in the IS-LM framework. You will learn to calculate the effects of fiscal policy changes, understand why fiscal policy is more effective in some circumstances than others, and evaluate the trade-offs between using fiscal vs. monetary policy to stabilize the economy.

core_concept.principle:
Fiscal policy in the IS-LM model works by shifting the IS curve. Expansionary fiscal policy (increasing $G$ or decreasing $T$) shifts the IS curve right, increasing output but also raising interest rates due to increased money demand. The interest rate increase partially offsets the output increase through crowding out of investment.

Government Spending Increase ($G \uparrow$):
• IS shifts right by $\Delta G \times \frac{1}{1-c}$ (horizontal shift at given $r$).
• New equilibrium: $Y \uparrow$, $r \uparrow$.
• Crowding out: The increase in $r$ reduces $I$, offsetting some of the output gain.
• General equilibrium multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = \frac{h}{h(1-c) + dk}$

Tax Cut ($T \downarrow$):
• IS shifts right by $(-\Delta T) \times \frac{c}{1-c}$ (horizontal shift at given $r$).
• Tax cuts are less powerful than spending increases because part of the tax cut is saved ($c < 1$).
• General equilibrium multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta T} = -\frac{ch}{h(1-c) + dk}$

Balanced Budget Multiplier ($\Delta G = \Delta T$):
• Simple balanced budget multiplier (fixed $r$): $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = 1$ (when $\Delta G = \Delta T$).
• In IS-LM (with crowding out): $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = \frac{h(1-c)}{h(1-c) + dk} < 1$ (for $\Delta G = \Delta T$).
• The balanced budget multiplier is positive but less than 1 in general equilibrium.

Crowding Out:
• The reduction in private investment caused by the increase in interest rates.
• Crowding out is larger when the LM curve is steeper ($h$ small or $k$ large).
• Crowding out is smaller when the LM curve is flatter ($h$ large or $k$ small).
• No crowding out in liquidity trap (horizontal LM).
• Full crowding out in classical case (vertical LM).

Effectiveness of Fiscal Policy:
• Fiscal policy is more effective when:
1. The LM curve is flat (liquidity trap, $h$ large)
2. The IS curve is steep ($d$ small, investment insensitive to interest rates)
3. The marginal propensity to consume ($c$) is large
4. The income sensitivity of money demand ($k$) is small
• Fiscal policy is less effective when the opposite conditions hold.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the government decides to increase spending on infrastructure by $\$100$ billion. In the Keynesian cross (fixed interest rate), this would increase output by $\$100$ billion × multiplier (say 5) = $\$500$ billion. But in the IS-LM model, the interest rate is not fixed. As output rises, people need more money for transactions. With a fixed money supply, the increased money demand pushes interest rates up. Higher interest rates reduce private investment (businesses borrow less, build fewer factories). This crowding out reduces the total increase in output. The actual increase might be only $\$300$ billion instead of $\$500$ billion.

How much crowding out occurs depends on how sensitive money demand is to interest rates. If money demand is very sensitive (flat LM), interest rates rise only a little, so crowding out is small. If money demand is insensitive (steep LM), interest rates rise a lot, and crowding out is large. In a liquidity trap (interest rates already at zero), money demand is perfectly elastic; interest rates do not rise at all, so there is no crowding out. This is why fiscal policy is so powerful during deep recessions when rates are at the zero lower bound.

Tax cuts work similarly, but they are less powerful than spending increases because households save part of the tax cut. If the MPC is 0.8, a $\$100$ billion tax cut increases autonomous spending by only $\$80$ billion (the rest is saved). So the IS curve shifts right by less than for a spending increase.

The balanced budget multiplier (increase G and T by the same amount) is interesting: the simple multiplier is 1 (output increases by exactly the increase in G). But with crowding out, the multiplier is less than 1 because the interest rate increase partially offsets the output gain.

image.alt: Graph: IS curve shifts right (G↑ or T↓); new equilibrium has higher Y and higher r (crowding out).
image.caption: Figure 9.5.1: Expansionary fiscal policy shifts the IS curve right, increasing output but also raising interest rates (crowding out).

sections.introduction:
Fiscal policy refers to changes in government spending (G) and taxes (T) designed to influence aggregate demand, output, and employment. In the IS-LM model, fiscal policy operates through the IS curve: an increase in government spending or a decrease in taxes shifts the IS curve to the right, increasing output at any given interest rate. However, unlike the simple Keynesian cross where interest rates are fixed, in the IS-LM model fiscal policy also affects interest rates through the money market. Higher output increases money demand, which raises interest rates (crowding out), partially offsetting the initial increase in output.

Understanding fiscal policy in the IS-LM model is essential for analyzing how government spending and tax changes affect the economy, evaluating the effectiveness of fiscal stimulus during recessions, and understanding the phenomenon of crowding out. The model shows that the multiplier for fiscal policy is smaller than the simple Keynesian multiplier because of crowding out, and that the effectiveness of fiscal policy depends on the slopes of the IS and LM curves. In a liquidity trap (horizontal LM), crowding out is zero and fiscal policy is very effective. In the classical case (vertical LM), crowding out is complete and fiscal policy is ineffective.

In this section, we will analyze the effects of government spending increases and tax cuts on output and interest rates, derive the fiscal policy multipliers, explore crowding out in detail, examine the balanced budget multiplier, compare the effectiveness of spending increases vs. tax cuts, and discuss the limitations of fiscal policy in the IS-LM framework.

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Simple multiplier (no crowding out) vs. general equilibrium multiplier (with crowding out); difference is crowding out.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 9.5.2: Fiscal policy multipliers: the simple multiplier (fixed interest rate) is larger than the general equilibrium multiplier (with crowding out).

key_concept.definition:
Fiscal Policy in IS-LM: Changes in government spending ($G$) or taxes ($T$) that shift the IS curve, affecting equilibrium output and interest rates.

Government Spending Multipliers:
• Simple multiplier (fixed $r$): $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = \frac{1}{1-c}$
• General equilibrium multiplier (with crowding out): $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = \frac{h}{h(1-c) + dk}$
• Crowding out = Simple multiplier - General equilibrium multiplier

Tax Multipliers:
• Simple tax multiplier (fixed $r$): $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta T} = -\frac{c}{1-c}$
• General equilibrium tax multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta T} = -\frac{ch}{h(1-c) + dk}$
• Tax cuts are less powerful than spending increases because $c < 1$.

Balanced Budget Multiplier ($\Delta G = \Delta T$):
• Simple balanced budget multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = 1$ (for $\Delta G = \Delta T$)
• General equilibrium balanced budget multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = \frac{h(1-c)}{h(1-c) + dk} < 1$

Crowding Out:
• Occurs because higher $Y$ increases money demand, raising $r$, which reduces $I$.
• Magnitude depends on slopes of IS and LM:
- Steep LM ($h$ small, $k$ large) → large crowding out
- Flat LM ($h$ large, $k$ small) → small crowding out
- Liquidity trap (horizontal LM) → zero crowding out
- Classical case (vertical LM) → full crowding out

Determinants of Fiscal Policy Effectiveness:
• Fiscal policy is more effective when:
- LM is flat (liquidity trap, $h$ large)
- IS is steep ($d$ small, investment insensitive to interest rates)
- MPC ($c$) is large
- Income sensitivity of money demand ($k$) is small
• Fiscal policy is less effective when the opposite conditions hold.
key_concept.context: The IS-LM model was used by Keynesian economists in the 1950s-1960s to argue for active fiscal policy. They believed that the LM curve was relatively flat (interest-sensitive money demand) and the IS curve was relatively steep (investment insensitive to interest rates), so crowding out was small and fiscal policy was effective. Monetarists (led by Milton Friedman) argued that the LM curve was steep (money demand interest-insensitive) and the IS curve was flat (investment interest-sensitive), so crowding out was large and fiscal policy was ineffective. The empirical evidence is mixed: during normal times, both policies work, but at the zero lower bound, fiscal policy is clearly more effective. The 2008-2009 fiscal stimulus (ARRA) and the 2020-2021 fiscal stimulus (CARES Act, ARP) were both effective, with estimates of the multiplier ranging from 0.5 to 2.0 depending on the economic conditions.

image.alt: Two graphs: Flat LM (fiscal policy effective, small crowding out) vs. Steep LM (fiscal policy ineffective, large crowding out).
image.caption: Figure 9.5.3: The effectiveness of fiscal policy depends on the slope of the LM curve. With a flat LM (liquidity trap), crowding out is minimal; with a steep LM, crowding out is large.

classifications.value: An increase in government spending shifts the IS curve right, increasing output but also raising interest rates (crowding out)
characteristics: IS shifts right by $\Delta G \times \frac{1}{1-c}$ (horizontal shift at given $r$).
Crowding out reduces investment by $d \times \Delta r$.
Effect is larger when LM is flatter (less crowding out) and smaller when LM is steeper (more crowding out).
classifications.behavior:
When the government increases spending, the IS curve shifts right. At the original interest rate, there is excess demand for goods. Output rises, but as output rises, money demand increases. With a fixed money supply, the increased money demand pushes interest rates up. Higher interest rates reduce investment (crowding out). The new equilibrium has higher output (but less than the simple multiplier would suggest) and higher interest rates.

The magnitude of crowding out depends on the slope of the LM curve. If the LM curve is flat (money demand very sensitive to interest rates, $h$ large), interest rates rise only a little, so crowding out is small, and fiscal policy is effective. If the LM curve is steep (money demand insensitive to interest rates, $h$ small), interest rates rise a lot, crowding out is large, and fiscal policy is less effective.

In the liquidity trap (horizontal LM, $h \rightarrow \infty$), there is no crowding out because interest rates do not rise. The multiplier equals the simple multiplier $1/(1-c)$. This is why fiscal stimulus is so powerful when the economy is at the zero lower bound.

In the classical case (vertical LM, $h = 0$), the multiplier is zero. Any increase in government spending is fully crowded out by a reduction in investment. Output does not increase at all; only interest rates rise.

image.alt: Graph: IS shifts right; new equilibrium at higher Y and higher r; crowding out shown as the difference between the simple multiplier and actual Y increase.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.4: An increase in government spending shifts the IS curve right, increasing output and interest rates. The output increase is smaller than the simple multiplier due to crowding out.

examples.explanation: The ARRA increased government spending by about $\$300$ billion. The Fed had cut rates to zero (liquidity trap), so the LM curve was horizontal. Crowding out was minimal. Estimates suggest the multiplier was 1.0-1.5, meaning output increased by $\$300-450$ billion. The interest rate did not rise because of the ZLB.

The Reagan administration increased defense spending. The Fed (Volcker) kept rates high to fight inflation, so the LM curve was steep. Crowding out was large. Private investment fell, and the net effect on output was smaller than the simple multiplier would suggest.

classifications.value: A tax cut shifts the IS curve right, but by less than an equivalent spending increase because households save part of the tax cut
characteristics: IS shifts right by $(-\Delta T) \times \frac{c}{1-c}$ (horizontal shift at given $r$).
Tax cuts are less powerful than spending increases because $c < 1$ (some of the tax cut is saved).
General equilibrium tax multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta T} = -\frac{ch}{h(1-c) + dk}$.
Absolute value of tax multiplier is $c$ times the spending multiplier.
The interest rate also rises (crowding out), reducing the effectiveness further.
classifications.behavior:
When the government cuts taxes, disposable income increases. Households consume a fraction $c$ (the MPC) of the tax cut and save the rest. The initial increase in autonomous spending is $c \times (-\Delta T)$. This shifts the IS curve right by $(-\Delta T) \times \frac{c}{1-c}$ (the simple multiplier times the initial spending increase).

As with government spending, the increase in output raises money demand, pushing interest rates up. Higher interest rates reduce investment, crowding out some of the output increase. The general equilibrium tax multiplier is $c$ times the spending multiplier.

Because $c < 1$, tax cuts are always less powerful than government spending increases as a stimulus tool. For example, if $c = 0.8$, a $\$100$ billion tax cut increases autonomous spending by only $\$80$ billion, while a $\$100$ billion spending increase adds $\$100$ billion directly. This is why Keynesians often prefer spending increases over tax cuts during recessions.

However, tax cuts can be implemented more quickly than spending projects (which take time to plan and execute). Tax cuts also have different distributional effects: they benefit households directly, while spending increases may benefit specific sectors (construction, defense, etc.).

image.alt: Graph: IS shifts right (T↓) but by less than for G↑; new equilibrium has higher Y and higher r.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.5: A tax cut shifts the IS curve right, but by less than an equivalent government spending increase because households save part of the tax cut.

examples.explanation: The 2001 tax cuts reduced taxes by about $\$100$ billion per year. With an MPC of 0.6-0.8, the initial spending increase was $\$60-80$ billion. The IS curve shifted right, but less than a spending increase of the same magnitude. Output increased, but the multiplier was modest (0.5-1.0). Interest rates rose slightly, causing some crowding out.

The CARES Act sent $\$1,200$ stimulus checks to most households. The MPC for low-income households is high (0.6-0.8), so the initial spending increase was significant. With the Fed at the ZLB, there was no crowding out. The tax cuts (transfers) were effective in boosting output.

classifications.value: When government spending and taxes increase by the same amount, the balanced budget multiplier is positive but less than 1 in general equilibrium
characteristics: Simple balanced budget multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = 1$ (when $\Delta G = \Delta T$, fixed $r$).
General equilibrium balanced budget multiplier: $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta G} = \frac{h(1-c)}{h(1-c) + dk} < 1$.
The multiplier is positive but less than 1 because of crowding out.

In a liquidity trap (horizontal LM), the balanced budget multiplier equals $1-c$ (since $h \rightarrow \infty$ gives $\Delta Y/\Delta G = 1-c$). Wait, check: $\lim_{h \to \infty} = (1-c)$? Actually $\frac{h(1-c)}{h(1-c)+dk} \to 1$ as $h \to \infty$, not $1-c$. So in liquidity trap, balanced budget multiplier = 1 (no crowding out).
The balanced budget multiplier is smaller than the simple spending multiplier because taxes reduce consumption.
classifications.behavior:
The balanced budget multiplier is the effect on output when government spending and taxes increase by the same amount ($\Delta G = \Delta T$). In the simple Keynesian cross (fixed interest rate), the balanced budget multiplier is exactly 1. Why? The spending increase adds $\Delta G$ directly to aggregate demand. The tax increase reduces consumption by $c \times \Delta T = c \times \Delta G$. The net effect is $\Delta G - c \Delta G = (1-c)\Delta G$. Then the multiplier amplifies this: $(1-c) \times \frac{1}{1-c} = 1$. So output increases by exactly the amount of the spending increase.

In the IS-LM model (with crowding out), the balanced budget multiplier is less than 1. The increase in output raises money demand, pushing interest rates up. Higher interest rates crowd out some investment, reducing the net output gain. The general equilibrium balanced budget multiplier is $\frac{h(1-c)}{h(1-c) + dk}$, which is always less than 1 (unless $d = 0$ or $h \rightarrow \infty$).

In a liquidity trap (horizontal LM, $h \rightarrow \infty$), the balanced budget multiplier approaches 1 (no crowding out). In the classical case (vertical LM, $h = 0$), the balanced budget multiplier is 0 (full crowding out).

image.alt: Graph: IS shifts right (G↑ and T↑); net shift smaller than G↑ alone; new equilibrium has higher Y and higher r.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.6: Balanced budget fiscal policy (ΔG = ΔT) shifts the IS curve right, but by less than a spending increase alone, because taxes reduce consumption.

examples.process: Balanced Budget Multiplier in Normal Times
examples.explanation: If the government increases spending and taxes by $\$100$ billion each, with $c=0.8$, $h=20$, $d=40$, $k=0.5$, the balanced budget multiplier is $\frac{20(0.2)}{20(0.2)+40(0.5)} = \frac{4}{4+20} = 4/24 = 0.167$. Output increases by only $\$16.7$ billion, much less than the $\$100$ billion increase in spending. Most of the spending is crowded out.

examples.process: Balanced Budget Multiplier in Liquidity Trap
examples.explanation: If the economy is in a liquidity trap ($h \rightarrow \infty$), the balanced budget multiplier approaches 1. Output increases by almost $\$100$ billion. There is no crowding out because interest rates do not rise.

principles.principle: Fiscal Policy Is More Effective When the LM Curve Is Flat (Liquidity Trap) and Less Effective When the LM Curve Is Steep (Classical Case)
principles.statement: The effectiveness of fiscal policy depends critically on the slope of the LM curve, which is determined by the interest sensitivity of money demand ($h$). When $h$ is large (flat LM), money demand is very sensitive to interest rates. An increase in output raises money demand, but only a small increase in interest rates is needed to choke off that demand. Therefore, interest rates rise only a little, crowding out is small, and fiscal policy is effective. When $h$ is small (steep LM), money demand is insensitive to interest rates. A large increase in interest rates is needed to restore money market equilibrium, leading to large crowding out and ineffective fiscal policy. In the extreme case of a liquidity trap ($h \rightarrow \infty$), the LM curve is horizontal, interest rates do not rise at all, and fiscal policy is maximally effective (multiplier equals the simple multiplier). In the classical case ($h = 0$), the LM curve is vertical, interest rates rise sharply, and fiscal policy is completely ineffective (full crowding out). This insight explains why Keynesians advocated fiscal stimulus during the Great Depression (liquidity trap) and why monetarists were skeptical of fiscal policy in normal times.
principles.application: Policymakers should assess the slope of the LM curve before implementing fiscal policy. If the economy is in a liquidity trap (interest rates near zero, money demand highly elastic), fiscal policy is very effective and should be used aggressively. If the economy is overheating (interest rates high, money demand less elastic), fiscal policy will be less effective due to crowding out, and monetary policy may be more appropriate. Central banks can influence the slope of the LM curve through their policy rate: when rates are near zero, the LM curve is flat; when rates are high, the LM curve is steeper.
principles.example: In 2009, the US was in a liquidity trap (fed funds rate at 0%). The LM curve was horizontal. Fiscal stimulus (ARRA) was effective, with multipliers estimated at 1.0-1.5. In 1994, the Fed was raising rates to prevent inflation. The LM curve was steep. A fiscal expansion at that time would have caused significant crowding out, reducing its effectiveness.

image.alt: Graph: Flat LM (small crowding out, large Y increase) vs. Steep LM (large crowding out, small Y increase).
image.caption: Figure 9.5.7: Fiscal policy is more effective when the LM curve is flat (liquidity trap) and less effective when the LM curve is steep.

principles.principle: Government Spending Increases Are More Powerful Than Tax Cuts of the Same Size
principles.statement: Because the marginal propensity to consume ($c$) is less than 1, a tax cut increases autonomous spending by only $c \times \Delta T$, while a government spending increase adds the full $\Delta G$ to autonomous spending. As a result, the multiplier for government spending is larger than the absolute value of the tax multiplier. For example, if $c = 0.8$, a $\$100$ billion spending increase has an initial effect of $\$100$ billion, while a $\$100$ billion tax cut has an initial effect of only $\$80$ billion. After the multiplier process, the spending increase raises output by $\$500$ billion (simple multiplier 5), while the tax cut raises output by $\$400$ billion. In general equilibrium (with crowding out), the same ratio holds: the spending multiplier is $1/(1-c)$ times the tax multiplier in absolute value. This implies that if the government wants to maximize the output impact of a given fiscal package, it should favor spending increases over tax cuts. However, tax cuts can be implemented more quickly, have different distributional effects, and may be politically more feasible.
principles.application: When designing fiscal stimulus, policymakers should consider the trade-off between spending increases and tax cuts. Spending increases have a larger multiplier but take longer to implement (inside lag). Tax cuts can be implemented quickly (e.g., stimulus checks) but have a smaller multiplier. The optimal mix depends on the urgency of the recession and the political environment. During the 2008 crisis, the ARRA included both spending (infrastructure, aid to states) and tax cuts (Making Work Pay credit). During the 2020 pandemic, the CARES Act focused on direct payments (tax cuts/transfers) because speed was critical.
principles.example: The 2009 ARRA included $\$300$ billion in spending and $\$200$ billion in tax cuts. The spending portion had an estimated multiplier of 1.5, while the tax cut portion had a multiplier of 1.0. The combined effect was larger than if the entire package had been tax cuts. The 2020 CARES Act included $\$300$ billion in direct payments (tax cuts/transfers) and $\$250$ billion in unemployment benefits (transfers). These had high multipliers (1.5-2.0) because they went to liquidity-constrained households with high MPCs.

image.alt: Graph: IS shift from G↑ (larger shift) vs. T↓ (smaller shift); Y increase larger for G↑.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.8: Government spending increases are more powerful than tax cuts of the same size because the entire spending increase enters aggregate demand, while only part of a tax cut is spent.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Effects of Government Spending and Tax Changes

image.alt: Table: c=0.8, d=40, k=0.5, h=20. G↑100, T↓100. Calculations of multipliers and output changes.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.9: Worked example: calculating the effects of government spending increases and tax cuts on output and interest rates.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has the following parameters:

• Marginal propensity to consume: $c = 0.8$
• Interest sensitivity of investment: $d = 40$
• Income sensitivity of money demand: $k = 0.5$
• Interest sensitivity of money demand: $h = 20$
• Initial equilibrium: $Y = 2000$, $r = 5\%$

a) Calculate the simple government spending multiplier (fixed interest rate).
b) Calculate the general equilibrium government spending multiplier (with crowding out).
c) If the government increases spending by $\Delta G = 100$, calculate the change in output ($\Delta Y$) and interest rate ($\Delta r$).
d) Calculate the simple tax multiplier and the general equilibrium tax multiplier.
e) If the government cuts taxes by $\Delta T = -100$, calculate the change in output ($\Delta Y$) and interest rate ($\Delta r$).
f) Compare the effectiveness of the spending increase vs. the tax cut.

step_by_step.step: Calculate ΔY and Δr for ΔG = 100
step_by_step.explanation:
$\Delta Y = 0.8333 \times 100 = 83.33$

To find $\Delta r$, use the LM curve slope: $\frac{\Delta r}{\Delta Y} = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.5}{20} = 0.025$

$\Delta r = 0.025 \times 83.33 = 2.08$ percentage points (208 basis points)

So $Y$ increases from 2000 to 2083.33, and $r$ increases from 5% to 7.08%.

Simple tax multiplier = $-\frac{c}{1-c} = -\frac{0.8}{0.2} = -4$

General equilibrium tax multiplier = $-\frac{ch}{h(1-c) + dk} = -\frac{0.8 \times 20}{24} = -\frac{16}{24} = -0.6667$

step_by_step.step: Calculate ΔY and Δr for ΔT = -100
step_by_step.explanation:
$\Delta Y = (-0.6667) \times (-100) = 66.67$

$\Delta r = 0.025 \times 66.67 = 1.67$ percentage points (167 basis points)

So $Y$ increases from 2000 to 2066.67, and $r$ increases from 5% to 6.67%.

step_by_step.step: Compare spending increase vs. tax cut
step_by_step.explanation:
A $\$100$ spending increase raises output by 83.33 and interest rates by 2.08%. A $\$100$ tax cut raises output by 66.67 and interest rates by 1.67%.

The spending increase is more powerful (83.33 > 66.67) because the entire spending increase enters aggregate demand directly, while only part of the tax cut is spent (the MPC is 0.8).

The ratio of the two output effects is $83.33 / 66.67 = 1.25$, which equals $1/c = 1/0.8 = 1.25$. Spending is 25% more powerful than a tax cut of the same size.

However, tax cuts may be implemented more quickly and can target households with high MPCs (e.g., low-income households). The optimal choice depends on the specific economic circumstances.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Simple spending multiplier = 5. b) General equilibrium spending multiplier = 0.8333. c) ΔY = 83.33, Δr = 2.08%. d) Simple tax multiplier = -4, general equilibrium tax multiplier = -0.6667. e) ΔY = 66.67, Δr = 1.67%. f) Spending increase is 25% more powerful than tax cut of same size.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying fiscal policy multipliers to calculate the effects of government spending and tax changes on output and interest rates.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Crowding Out Under Different LM Slopes

image.alt: Table: Scenario A (h=20, flat LM) vs. Scenario B (h=5, steep LM); crowding out calculations.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.10: Worked example: comparing crowding out under flat vs. steep LM curves.

worked_examples.problem:
Compare the effects of a $\Delta G = 100$ government spending increase under two different money demand regimes:

Scenario A (Flat LM): $h = 20$, $k = 0.5$, $c = 0.8$, $d = 40$
Scenario B (Steep LM): $h = 5$, $k = 0.5$, $c = 0.8$, $d = 40$

a) Calculate the general equilibrium spending multiplier for each scenario.
b) Calculate the change in output ($\Delta Y$) for each scenario.
c) Calculate the change in interest rate ($\Delta r$) for each scenario.
d) Calculate the amount of crowding out (simple multiplier minus general equilibrium multiplier) for each scenario.
e) Explain why crowding out is larger in Scenario B.
f) If the economy were in a liquidity trap ($h \rightarrow \infty$), what would be the multiplier and crowding out?

step_by_step.explanation:
General equilibrium multiplier formula: $\frac{h}{h(1-c) + dk}$

Scenario A ($h = 20$):
$\frac{20}{20(0.2) + 40(0.5)} = \frac{20}{4 + 20} = \frac{20}{24} = 0.8333$

Scenario B ($h = 5$):
$\frac{5}{5(0.2) + 40(0.5)} = \frac{5}{1 + 20} = \frac{5}{21} = 0.2381$

Scenario A: $\Delta Y = 0.8333 \times 100 = 83.33$

Scenario B: $\Delta Y = 0.2381 \times 100 = 23.81$

Fiscal policy is much more effective in Scenario A (flat LM) than in Scenario B (steep LM).

$\Delta r = \frac{k}{h} \times \Delta Y$

Scenario A: $\frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.5}{20} = 0.025$
$\Delta r = 0.025 \times 83.33 = 2.08\%$

Scenario B: $\frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.5}{5} = 0.10$
$\Delta r = 0.10 \times 23.81 = 2.38\%$

Interest rates rise more in Scenario B (2.38% vs. 2.08%) even though output increases less. This is because the LM curve is steeper.

Simple multiplier = 5. Crowding out = simple multiplier - general equilibrium multiplier.

Scenario A: $5 - 0.8333 = 4.1667$

Scenario B: $5 - 0.2381 = 4.7619$

Crowding out is larger in Scenario B (4.76 vs. 4.17).

step_by_step.step: Explain why crowding out is larger in Scenario B
step_by_step.explanation:
Crowding out is larger in Scenario B because the LM curve is steeper ($h$ is smaller). A steeper LM curve means that an increase in output causes a larger increase in interest rates ($\Delta r/\Delta Y = k/h$ is larger). Higher interest rates reduce investment more, offsetting more of the initial output increase.

In Scenario A ($h = 20$), the LM curve is flatter, so interest rates rise only a little (2.08% for an 83.33 output increase). The crowding out effect is smaller.

Mathematically, the denominator of the multiplier is $h(1-c) + dk$. When $h$ is smaller, the denominator is smaller? Actually $h(1-c)$ is smaller, but the multiplier formula is $h$ divided by that denominator. As $h$ decreases, the numerator decreases proportionally, but the denominator decreases less because $dk$ is constant. The net effect is a smaller multiplier (more crowding out).

If $h \rightarrow \infty$ (liquidity trap, horizontal LM), the general equilibrium multiplier approaches the simple multiplier:

$\lim_{h \to \infty} \frac{h}{h(1-c) + dk} = \lim_{h \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-c) + (dk)/h} = \frac{1}{1-c} = 5$

Crowding out = $5 - 5 = 0$

In a liquidity trap, there is no crowding out because interest rates do not rise (the LM curve is horizontal). Fiscal policy is maximally effective. This is why the 2009 ARRA and 2020 CARES Act were effective despite large government borrowing.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Scenario A: 0.8333, Scenario B: 0.2381. b) A: 83.33, B: 23.81. c) A: Δr = 2.08%, B: Δr = 2.38%. d) A: 4.17, B: 4.76. e) Steeper LM (smaller h) causes larger interest rate increases, more crowding out. f) In liquidity trap, multiplier = 5, crowding out = 0.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the general equilibrium multiplier to compare crowding out under different LM slopes and understanding why fiscal policy is more effective when the LM curve is flat.

process_flow.process: Determine the fiscal policy action (ΔG or ΔT)
process_flow.description: Identify whether the government is increasing spending, cutting taxes, or a combination.
process_flow.mechanism: ΔG > 0 (spending increase), ΔT < 0 (tax cut), or balanced budget (ΔG = ΔT).

image.alt: Diagram: Policy choice (G↑, T↓, or both).
image.caption: Figure 9.5.11: Step 1 - Determine the fiscal policy action (government spending increase, tax cut, or balanced budget).

process_flow.process: Calculate the horizontal shift of the IS curve
process_flow.description: The IS curve shifts right by the change in autonomous spending times the simple multiplier.
process_flow.mechanism: For ΔG: shift = ΔG × 1/(1-c). For ΔT: shift = (-ΔT) × c/(1-c).

image.caption: Figure 9.5.12: Step 2 - Calculate the horizontal shift of the IS curve based on the fiscal policy change.

process_flow.process: Find new equilibrium (intersection of new IS with LM)
process_flow.description: The new IS curve intersects the LM curve at a new point with higher Y and higher r.
process_flow.mechanism: Solve IS and LM simultaneously or use multiplier formulas.

image.alt: Graph: New IS curve (shifted right) intersects LM at higher Y and higher r.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.13: Step 3 - Find the new equilibrium where the shifted IS curve intersects the LM curve.

process_flow.description: The increase in interest rates reduces investment, partially offsetting the output increase.
process_flow.mechanism: Crowding out = d × Δr (where d is interest sensitivity of investment).

image.alt: Equation: Crowding out = d × Δr; ΔI = -d × Δr.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.14: Step 4 - Calculate crowding out: the reduction in investment due to higher interest rates.

process_flow.process: Compare with simple multiplier to assess effectiveness
process_flow.description: Compare the actual output increase (with crowding out) to the simple multiplier prediction (no crowding out).
process_flow.mechanism: Effectiveness ratio = actual ΔY / simple multiplier ΔY. Lower ratio means more crowding out.

image.alt: Table: Simple multiplier vs. actual multiplier; crowding out = difference.
image.caption: Figure 9.5.15: Step 5 - Compare the actual output increase to the simple multiplier to assess the extent of crowding out.

image.alt: Master framework: Fiscal policy action (G↑ or T↓) → IS shift → new equilibrium (Y↑, r↑) → crowding out (I↓) → net Y increase (less than simple multiplier).
image.caption: Figure 9.5.16: Complete framework for fiscal policy in the IS-LM model: from policy action to equilibrium and crowding out.

summary: Fiscal policy in the IS-LM model operates through shifts of the IS curve. An increase in government spending ($G$) or a decrease in taxes ($T$) shifts the IS curve right, increasing output but also raising interest rates due to increased money demand. Higher interest rates reduce private investment, a phenomenon called crowding out, which partially offsets the initial output increase. The general equilibrium spending multiplier is $\frac{h}{h(1-c) + dk}$, which is smaller than the simple multiplier $\frac{1}{1-c}$. The tax multiplier is $-c$ times the spending multiplier. The balanced budget multiplier ($\Delta G = \Delta T$) is positive but less than 1 in general equilibrium. Crowding out is larger when the LM curve is steep (small $h$) and smaller when the LM curve is flat (large $h$). In a liquidity trap (horizontal LM), there is no crowding out, and fiscal policy is maximally effective (multiplier equals the simple multiplier). In the classical case (vertical LM), crowding out is complete, and fiscal policy is ineffective. Government spending increases are more powerful than tax cuts of the same size because the entire spending increase enters aggregate demand, while only part of a tax cut is spent (the MPC is less than 1). Understanding fiscal policy in the IS-LM model is essential for analyzing the effects of stimulus packages, evaluating the trade-offs between spending and tax cuts, and understanding when fiscal policy is most effective.
